summary: Cantrill argues that the tech industry has allowed software vendors to capture the economic benefits of hardware improvements through per-core licensing, a practice that has become especially egregious as CPU advances now manifest primarily in core count rather than clock speed. He traces the shift from Amazon's customer-friendly price cuts to margin-maximizing depreciation extensions, and criticizes the PC-era separation of hardware and software that enables enterprise vendors to tax infrastructure improvements. Oxide's approach of including all software with hardware purchases is positioned as returning Moore's Law dividends to their rightful owners: the users of compute.
key_points: Cloud providers have shifted from passing hardware improvements to customers via price cuts to capturing them through extended depreciation
Per-core software licensing allows third parties to tax infrastructure improvements they neither developed nor paid for
The hardware/software divide in enterprise computing creates finger-pointing and prevents beneficial co-design
Software costs can absorb entire gains from next-generation CPUs, creating disincentives to upgrade infrastructure
Oxide includes all software with hardware purchases, ensuring hardware improvements benefit users
money_quotes: Per-core licensing allows a third party—who neither made the significant investment in developing the next generation of microprocessor nor paid for the part themselves—to exact a tax on improved infrastructure.
Software costs can potentially absorb the entire gain from a next-generation CPU, leaving a disincentive to run newer, more efficient infrastructure.

In the go-go era of Dennard scaling (when clock rates rose at a blistering rate), software vendors never would have been allowed to get away with charging by the gigahertz; we should not allow them to feel so emboldened to charge by core count now!
This gives the dividends of Moore's Law to whom they rightfully belong: the users of compute.
And yes, it's all open source—which unfortunately demands the immediate clarification that it's actually open source rather than pretend open source.
key_insight: Per-core software licensing is an industry-accepted practice that unjustly allows software vendors to capture the economic benefits of hardware innovation that rightfully belong to the users who purchased that hardware.
